target: data/sections.json part5.html — SEC-5 Kill-Shot Binary Tests: add strength-rating badges to each test header (ISS-937) and expand Test 5 Eclipse 2026 stub into a self-contained kill-shot (ISS-941).
curmudgeon_review: n/a (decider-assigned batch; derived from ISS-937 and ISS-941 direct-observation issues, not a curmudgeon review)
kernel_of_truth: Two kernels. (1) For ISS-937: the Kill-Shot page is carrying a real claim about evidence hierarchy "3 decisive, 2 major, 2 supportive" and our own Strength Rating Key exists specifically to license it; the original author of that block did the epistemic work of distinguishing "settles it alone" from "adds corroborating weight" and that distinction is genuinely useful, not window dressing. The gap is only that the per-test rating was never surfaced to the reader. (2) For ISS-941: the dome's August 2026 eclipse prediction is actually the dome's best-engineered kill-shot candidate — it has pre-registered timestamps, per-station magnitudes, error bars, and four discriminating sub-tests. Giving Section 4.2 all the work and leaving Test 5 as a deferral paragraph understates both how seriously the dome tried (which we should acknowledge) and how cleanly the prediction still fails (geometry borrowed from the globe, formula engineered to land inside Chapman). Writing the kill-shot directly in Test 5 lets the reader see both facts at once.

test1_sydney_perth.rationale: V13 Finsler formula is calibrated against the known Indian Pacific railway distance (curve-fitting) and two internal methods (Finsler vs MDS) disagree by 460 km. Strong multi-faceted argument, but it requires the reader to engage with methodology details; a defender could still argue V13 is legitimately predictive. Not binary.

test2_polaris.rationale: Single unreplicated measurement; claimed +0.27 deg excess sits inside Polaris' own polar offset (+-0.66 deg) and typical field uncertainty (+-0.2-0.5 deg). The strongest component is the dome's own FAIL-001 internal contradiction, but that contradiction is corroborating context rather than a standalone resolution.

test3_jfk_lhr.rationale: Three independent discriminating sub-tests (seasonal variation, equatorial absence, Southern Hemisphere reversal) all match jet stream physics and none match fixed-aether. The dome's own AI Context file Rule 15 admits the mechanism is relabeling. Binary on the underlying physics; no interpretive escape.

test4_saa.rationale: Non-discriminating: both dome and globe models predict continued SAA weakening from the same CHAOS-7 input data. The test produces corroborating weight against dome uniqueness claims but cannot independently resolve the question.

test5_eclipse.rationale: Multi-layered kill-shot: (a) eclipse path requires JPL DE440/441 ephemerides computed on globe Keplerian orbits, (b) magnetic dip prediction lands inside Chapman 5-25 nT by construction, (c) three inconsistent dome values (-17 to -21, -28.9, -29.1 nT) undermine claimed precision. Requires reader to follow multiple independent lines, not binary on a single measurement.

test6_nmp_drift.rationale: 39.9% error on the dome's own stated metric; the dome's formula predicts the error grows in 2027 and 2028. Binary fail against the model's own falsification threshold, no interpretive escape. The dome already self-reports this as the failure state.

test7_gaia.rationale: Dome predicts uniform 0.454 arcsec parallax for all stars from a 20m firmament wobble; Gaia DR3 observes parallax varying by a factor of 760 across 1.8 billion stars, tracking distance as heliocentric geometry requires. Proxima Centauri alone rejects the dome prediction at 6,281 sigma. Binary on the measurement.

strength_rating_assignments.totals: 3 decisive (Tests 3, 6, 7) + 2 major (Tests 1, 5) + 2 supportive (Tests 2, 4) = 7. Matches the aggregate claim in the Strength Rating Key exactly.

patches.description: Replace the Test 5 single-paragraph stub with a three-paragraph self-contained kill-shot argument (ISS-941). Geometric kill-shot, Chapman non-discrimination, link to Section 4.2 retained for depth.
patches.old_text: This test is addressed in detail in Section 4.2. The dome registers a magnetic anomaly prediction for the August 12, 2026 eclipse: −17 to −21 nT at Ebro (summarized from a dual-baseline system spanning −5 to −26 nT across 9 INTERMAGNET stations). The eclipse’s geographic path is defined by standard Besselian element calculations (JPL DE440/441 ephemerides) — the dome’s local-sun geometry does not independently produce eclipse paths. For the magnetic anomaly prediction itself, the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions, making the prediction non-discriminating. See Section 4.2 for the full analysis.
patches.new_text:
Credit where due. The August 12, 2026 eclipse prediction is the most methodologically careful element in the entire dome registry: nine named INTERMAGNET stations, per-station magnitudes and error bars, two separate baselines, and git-committed pre-registration before the event. Among kill-shot candidates, this one at least tries to state a prior commitment on observable data. The flaw is not lack of care; it is what the formula is actually doing underneath.

The prior geometric kill-shot. Before the magnetic dip becomes testable, the eclipse path itself is already borrowed from the globe. The August 2026 track — Greenland, Iceland, northern Spain, then the Mediterranean — is computed from Besselian elements fed by JPL’s DE440/441 ephemeris, which solves the Sun–Moon–Earth problem under heliocentric Keplerian orbits with a Sun at roughly 150 million km and a Moon at roughly 384,000 km. The dome has a local sun at 5,733 km above a flat 20,015-km-radius disc and a local moon at 2,534 km. On that geometry the shadow cast by the near moon on the flat disc is an ellipse swept by local projective geometry, not the great-circle arc that matches the observed eclipse track. The dome registry supplies no independently derived Besselian elements of its own; it takes the globe-computed path and then runs its magnetic formula against the station-by-station coverage values that path produces. The eclipse geometry is granted by the globe before the magnetic “prediction” begins.

The magnetic prediction is non-discriminating by construction. The dome formula ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF multiplies a 2017 Boulder Observatory measurement (a real globe-era INTERMAGNET baseline) by the globe-computed per-station coverage (0.40–0.95) and an unpublished “Field Strength Factor” (0.642–2.075). Plugged in, the predicted dips at Ebro are reported in the dome’s own documents as three inconsistent values — −17 to −21, −28.9, and −29.1 nT — which already undermines the claimed precision. But even the central figure falls inside the 5–25 nT range that Chapman’s 1933 ionospheric mechanism predicts under identical eclipse conditions from standard globe physics, with no dome geometry involved. A prediction engineered from globe-era baselines, globe-computed coverage, and a free-parameter multiplier cannot distinguish the dome from the globe — it lands inside Chapman by construction, regardless of how the event turns out. See Section 4.2 for the per-station numbers, the BOU/PIN dual-baseline analysis, and the four model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D).

holes_addressed: ISS-937: Strength Rating Key previously asserted "3 decisive, 2 major, 2 supportive" with no visible per-test badges. After this patch each test header carries an inline-styled ks-rating badge (Decisive / Major / Supportive) so the reader can count directly, and the aggregate sentence is updated to explicitly list which tests fall in which tier.

ISS-941: Test 5 (Eclipse 2026) was a single-paragraph deferral to Section 4.2. After this patch Test 5 carries a self-contained three-paragraph kill-shot: (1) credit where due for the dome's pre-registered methodology, (2) the geometric-impossibility argument that the eclipse path itself is computed by JPL DE440/441 on globe Keplerian orbits, and (3) the non-discrimination argument that the magnetic-dip prediction lands inside Chapman 5-25 nT by construction with three inconsistent dome values. Section 4.2 is still linked for per-station numbers and the four discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D), so this is addition not duplication.
new_evidence_added: Explicit per-test strength ratings derived from verdict text and evidence weight (see strength_rating_assignments block).
Eclipse path JPL DE440/441 ephemeris dependence, spelled out for Test 5 without forcing the reader into Section 4.2.
Local moon at 2,534 km above flat 20,015-km-radius disc vs Moon at ~384,000 km in globe Keplerian geometry, as the specific geometric incompatibility.
Three inconsistent dome magnetic-dip values (-17 to -21, -28.9, -29.1 nT) surfaced directly in Test 5, not only in Section 4.2.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Strength Rating assignments are subjective. Why is Sydney-Perth "Major" but JFK-LHR "Decisive" when both are multi-faceted arguments?
anticipated_objections.response: The Key defines Decisive as "binary on the underlying measurement, regardless of post-hoc reinterpretations of the threshold or conditions." JFK-LHR is binary on three independent direct observations (seasonal/equatorial/Southern-reversal), each of which matches jet stream and fails fixed aether; the dome's own AI Context Rule 15 concedes the mechanism is relabeling. Sydney-Perth is Major because the argument is about methodology (curve-fitting to a known answer) rather than a binary observational outcome — a committed defender could still claim V13 is legitimately predictive, even after the V12 78% failure is shown. Rating the two the same would collapse a real distinction the Key explicitly draws.

anticipated_objections.objection: Test 5 now duplicates content from Section 4.2 — the reader sees the same geometric and Chapman arguments twice.
anticipated_objections.response: Test 5 and Section 4.2 now have distinct roles. Test 5 states the kill-shot compactly: credit for pre-registered methodology, geometry borrowed from the globe, prediction lands inside Chapman by construction. Section 4.2 carries the detail load: the per-station magnitudes, BOU vs PIN dual-baseline analysis, FSF formula derivation, and the four discriminating sub-tests E-PRED-A through D. The Test 5 paragraphs deliberately do not reproduce the per-station numbers or the sub-test breakdown — they summarize the kill-shot and link forward for readers who want the full evidence.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding coloured badges and tier labels is visual noise. The header already has a status badge and a verdict badge.
anticipated_objections.response: The Strength Rating Key was already making a three-tier aggregate claim (3 decisive, 2 major, 2 supportive). Without a per-test badge, that claim is unverifiable — the reader has no way to know which tests we rated as which, and CLAUDE.md explicitly flags hardcoded aggregate counts as a review quality issue. The badge is the minimum-friction fix: it surfaces the information that was already implicit in the page without restructuring the table or the key block. Alternative — removing the aggregate sentence entirely — throws away a genuinely useful distinction the Key spent a paragraph defining.

anticipated_objections.objection: Saying the eclipse path is "borrowed from the globe" is question-begging if the dome rejects the globe frame entirely. Why is this a kill-shot rather than a framing disagreement?
anticipated_objections.response: The dome registry itself cites JPL ephemerides and Besselian elements as the source for the eclipse path in its own documents — it does not produce an independent dome-derived eclipse track. This is not our imposition of the globe frame onto the dome; it is the dome's documented practice. A model that depends on globe computations for its input geometry and then applies a formula engineered from globe-era baseline measurements cannot claim to discriminate against the globe on the output of that formula. The kill-shot is not "dome geometry is wrong because globe geometry says so"; it is "dome geometry is never actually used here; the globe is doing the work."

tldr_check: Test 5 TLDR already reads: "The Chapman ionospheric mechanism (1933) already predicts 5-25 nT, fully covering the dome's claimed range. The eclipse path itself requires JPL DE440/441 ephemerides - globe Keplerian orbits. Three inconsistent dome values (-17 to -21, -28.9, -29.1 nT) undermine the claimed precision." This already matches the expanded body; no TLDR change needed.
css_note: The patches use inline styles for .ks-rating and its variants (.ks-rating-decisive / .ks-rating-major / .ks-rating-supportive). No CSS changes are required, since each badge ships its own style attribute, matching the existing .verdict-badge pattern. If the decider wants to centralise these styles later, the class names are already in place for a future CSS patch.
integration_notes: The "Current ratings" sentence still hard-codes the 3/2/2 tallies (it now spells out which tests fall in which tier). This is acceptable because the totals are verifiable from the badges on each test header, but if a future test is added or a rating changes, all four spots must be updated in lock-step: (a) the new test header badge, (b) the explicit (Tests ...) list in the "Current ratings" sentence, (c) the aggregate 3/2/2 numbers, (d) the KS_DECISIVE/KS_MAJOR/KS_SUPPORTIVE HTML comments (currently unused but left in place). A follow-up issue to compute this from sections.json (e.g. via template variables driven off ks-rating classes) would remove that coupling.
fallback: If any PATCH-1 through PATCH-7 fails because the header markup in sections.json has changed since this expansion was written, the decider should attempt the minimum-friction alternative from ISS-937: remove the "Current ratings: 3 decisive, 2 major, 2 supportive" sentence entirely rather than ship an unverified aggregate claim. PATCH-9 (Test 5 body) is independent and should still apply even if the header patches need rework.
